candidate_problems.text: How can the conjecture non-circularly specify which transformations, invariants, and viability conditions are 'semantically relevant' rather than merely useful to an outside observer? The core preservation condition depends on a privileged class of T_W, T_I and on system viability, but it remains open how these are selected from within the physics of the system without smuggling in intentional notions or observer-relative interests.

What principled criterion distinguishes genuine representation from sophisticated causal control in systems that satisfy compression, robustness, and action-guidance? Many non-mental systems (thermostats, regulators, morphogenetic networks, machine-learning controllers) may instantiate stable world-internal-action mappings, so the conjecture needs a sharper boundary condition explaining when preserved structure is sufficient for aboutness and when it is only functional covariance.

What exact mathematical account of misrepresentation, error, and normativity follows from the proposed relational structure? The conjecture says error occurs when an internal state continues to play its role despite no current world state satisfying R, but it is unresolved how to define correctness conditions, false content, and degrees of representational success across counterfactual worlds in a way that is objective, tractable, and not reducible to mere causal malfunction.
